title: Dynamic casting

exercises.title: Safe downcast with dynamic_cast
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Use `dynamic_cast<Derived*>` to safely determine the actual type of objects accessed through base pointers, and call a derived-only function when the cast succeeds.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) -- the number of objects.
- Next `N` lines: either `base` or `derived`.

## Output

For each object, print one line:
- If the object is a `Base`, the `dynamic_cast` to `Derived*` fails. Print: `Cast failed: base object`
- If the object is a `Derived`, the cast succeeds. Print: `Cast succeeded: <message>` where `<message>` is the return value of `Derived::special()`.

`Derived::special()` returns `"I am Derived"`.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with a virtual destructor (use `= default`).
- Define a class `Derived` that publicly inherits from `Base`. Add a non-virtual member function `std::string special() const` that returns `"I am Derived"`.
- In `main`, read `N`, then for each line create either a `Base` or `Derived` on the heap (via `new`), storing the result in a `Base*`.
- For each `Base*`, use `dynamic_cast<Derived*>` to attempt a downcast. If the result is not `nullptr`, print `Cast succeeded: ` followed by calling `special()` on the resulting pointer. Otherwise print `Cast failed: base object`.
- Delete each object after processing it.

test_cases.label: Mixed base and derived
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast failed: base object
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast failed: base object

test_cases.label: All derived
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast succeeded: I am Derived

exercises.title: Counting derived types in a polymorphic collection
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a three-level hierarchy (`Animal` -> `Dog`, `Animal` -> `Cat`) and use `dynamic_cast` to count how many objects in a polymorphic collection are of a specific derived type. Then call a derived-only function on each successfully cast object.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 20) -- the number of animals.
- Next `N` lines: either `dog <name>` or `cat <name>`, where `<name>` is a single word (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Last line: a string, either `dog` or `cat`, indicating which type to search for.

## Output

- For each animal that matches the search type (via `dynamic_cast`), print the result of calling the type-specific function:
  - For `Dog`: `Dog: <name> says Woof`
  - For `Cat`: `Cat: <name> says Meow`
- After all matches, print: `Found: <count>` where `<count>` is the number of successful casts.

If no animals match, print only `Found: 0`.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a `std::string m_name` member, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a virtual destructor (`= default`), and a public accessor `const std::string& getName() const`.
- Define a class `Dog` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. Its constructor forwards the name. Add a non-virtual member function `std::string bark() const` that returns `"Dog: " + getName() + " says Woof"`.
- Define a class `Cat` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. Its constructor forwards the name. Add a non-virtual member function `std::string meow() const` that returns `"Cat: " + getName() + " says Meow"`.
- In `main`, read all animals into a `std::vector<Animal*>` (allocated with `new`).
- Read the search type. Loop over the vector and use `dynamic_cast` to attempt casting each `Animal*` to the target type. On success, call the appropriate function (`bark()` or `meow()`) and print the result. Count successes.
- Print `Found: <count>` at the end.
- Delete all animals before exiting.

test_cases.label: Find dogs in mixed collection
test_cases.stdin:
4
dog Rex
cat Whiskers
dog Buddy
cat Luna
dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Dog: Rex says Woof
Dog: Buddy says Woof
Found: 2

test_cases.label: Find cats in all-cat collection
test_cases.stdin:
3
cat Milo
cat Nala
cat Simba
cat
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cat: Milo says Meow
Cat: Nala says Meow
Cat: Simba says Meow
Found: 3
